content:
5 min read
Just now

--

Most news websites expect users to read complete articles, but in today’s fast-paced world, people often just want the key information. Inspired by Inshorts, I built a web application that fetches the latest news, summarizes it using AI, and presents it in a clean, easy-to-read interface.

Introduction
While learning JavaScript, I wanted to build something that would challenge me beyond creating static web pages. Instead of using frameworks like React, I decided to use Vanilla JavaScript and organize the entire project using the MVC (Model-View-Controller) architecture.
The idea was simple:

• Fetch the latest news from NewsAPI.

• Use an AI model to summarize each article.

• Display concise news cards similar to the Inshorts application.
This project helped me understand how real-world web applications are structured and how different components communicate with each other.

Project Overview
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Press enter or click to view image in full size

The application provides users with:

• AI-generated summaries of news articles

• Multiple news categories

• Responsive user interface

• Original article link through the “Read More” button

• Sidebar navigation for switching categories
Instead of displaying long descriptions, every article is summarized into a concise paragraph using an AI model, allowing users to quickly understand the main story.

Technologies Used
Frontend

• HTML5

• CSS3

• Vanilla JavaScript (ES6 Modules)
Architecture

• MVC (Model-View-Controller)
APIs

• NewsAPI

• Groq API (Llama 3.3 70B)
JavaScript Concepts

• Async/Await

• Fetch API

• DOM Manipulation

• ES Modules

• Object-Oriented Programming

• Event Handling

Project Structure
To keep the project organized, I followed the MVC architecture:
Separating responsibilities made the application much easier to understand and maintain.

MVC Architecture
Model
The News Model acts as a blueprint for every news article.
Instead of working directly with the raw API response, every article is converted into a News object containing information such as:

• Title

• Description

• Content

• Author

• Published Date

• Image URL

• Article URL
This ensures every article has a consistent structure throughout the application.

Service
The Service Layer is responsible for communicating with external APIs.
Its responsibilities include:

• Fetching news from NewsAPI

• Converting API responses into News objects

• Storing news category-wise

• Returning processed data to the controller
The service layer keeps all API-related logic in one place.

Controller
The controller acts as the coordinator of the application.
It is responsible for:

• Loading news when the page opens

• Handling sidebar category changes

• Calling the AI summarization function

• Rendering news cards

• Managing user interactions
Instead of directly manipulating the UI, the controller delegates rendering to the view.

View
The generateCards() function works as the View.
Its job is simple:

• Receive an array of news objects.

• Generate HTML dynamically.

• Display responsive news cards.
Keeping rendering separate from business logic made the application much cleaner.

How AI Summarization Works
One of the most interesting parts of the project was integrating an AI model.
The application follows this pipeline:
Page Loads
↓
Fetch News from NewsAPI
↓
Create News Objects
↓
Send Title + Description + Content to Groq AI
↓
Receive AI Summaries
↓
Replace Original Description
↓
Generate News CardsInstead of asking the AI to generate entirely new content, I only send:

• Title

• Description

• Content
The AI returns the same JSON structure, with only the description replaced by a concise summary of around 50–60 words.
This made the integration straightforward while keeping the original article data intact.

Challenges I Faced
1. Incomplete Article Content
NewsAPI often returns truncated content ending with: …[+1234 chars]
To improve summary quality, I combined the article’s title, description, and content before sending it to the AI model.

2. AI Response Validation
Large language models don’t always return perfectly formatted JSON.
If the AI response isn’t valid, the application automatically falls back to the original descriptions from NewsAPI instead of breaking the interface.

3. Reducing Unnecessary API Calls
Initially, every time a category was opened, the application fetched the news again.
I improved this by storing articles category-wise in memory.
Now, if a category has already been loaded once, the application simply displays the cached data instead of making another API request.
This reduces loading time and unnecessary API usage.

4. Missing Images
Some articles didn’t contain valid image URLs.
To maintain a consistent layout, I displayed a default fallback image whenever an article image was unavailable.

What I Learned
Building this project helped me understand much more than just JavaScript syntax.
Some of the key concepts I practiced include:

• MVC Architecture

• Separation of Concerns

• Object-Oriented Programming

• API Integration

• Async Programming using Async/Await

• DOM Manipulation

• AI API Integration

• Error Handling

• Code Organization

• Caching Data in Memory
More importantly, I learned how structuring an application properly makes it much easier to add new features without affecting existing code.

Future Improvements
There are several features I would like to add in future versions:

• Search functionality

• Infinite scrolling

• Bookmark articles

• Dark mode

• Backend integration to securely store API keys

• Automatic news refresh

• Multi-language AI summaries

Conclusion
This project was much more than recreating the Inshorts interface.
It gave me practical experience in building a real-world web application using Vanilla JavaScript, organizing code with the MVC architecture, integrating multiple APIs, and using Generative AI to enhance the user experience.
Working on this project reinforced the importance of writing modular, maintainable code and showed me how software architecture plays a significant role even in relatively small applications.
As I continue learning full-stack development, I plan to extend this project further by adding backend support, user authentication, and more intelligent AI-powered features.
